title: 6.9 practice
description: Practice what you learned in lesson 6.9!
topic_path: The First Global Age (1200 to 1750 CE)

assessment_items.question_content:
**How was the Transatlantic Slave Trade different from many earlier forms of slavery?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: It treated enslaved people strictly as property to be bought, sold, and inherited.
It involved people working in exchange for future land ownership.
It only targeted prisoners of war for temporary labor contracts.
It was mostly a local system that did not affect global trade networks.

assessment_items.question_content:
**In what ways did the demand for enslaved people fuel war among African communities during the Transatlantic Slave Trade?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: African states competed for European trade by waging wars to capture people for sale.
African kingdoms united in peace to resist the slave trade through diplomacy.
Europeans forced African kingdoms to fight one another directly.
The slave trade caused African communities to isolate themselves and avoid contact with others.

assessment_items.question_content:
**How was the scale of the Transatlantic Slave Trade different from earlier forms of slavery?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: It involved millions of people over several centuries, making it the largest forced migration in history.
It only enslaved prisoners of war from local conflicts within small regions.
It was limited to a short period and a small number of enslaved people.
It primarily involved voluntary labor agreements instead of forced enslavement.

assessment_items.question_content:
>I was soon put down under the decks, and there I received such a salutation [greeting] in my nostrils as I had never experienced in my life: so that, with the loathsomeness of the stench and crying together, I became so sick and low that I was not able to eat.

>— Olaudah Equiano, *The Interesting Narrative of the Life of Olaudah Equiano,* 1789


**What does this passage reveal about the conditions enslaved Africans faced during the Middle Passage?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: They experienced extreme suffering and harsh conditions below deck.
The crew treated them with kindness and care.
They were allowed to eat and move freely throughout the ship.
They found the journey bearable despite the challenges.

assessment_items.question_content:
>The decks were so crowded that each had scarcely room to turn himself... The air soon became unfit for respiration, from a variety of loathsome smells, and brought on a sickness among the slaves, of which many died.  

>— Alexander Falconbridge, British surgeon and former slave ship doctor, An Account of the Slave Trade on the Coast of Africa (1788)


**Based on this passage, what conclusion can be drawn about European awareness of slave ship conditions?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Some Europeans documented and exposed the brutal realities of the Middle Passage.
European observers were unaware of the poor conditions on slave ships.
European sailors prioritized the health and comfort of enslaved Africans.
The Middle Passage was a safe and efficient transportation method for labor.

assessment_items.question_content:
The image below is of the British slave ship Brookes (1788). 

[[☃ image 1]]

**What conclusion does this diagram support about the conditions endured by enslaved Africans aboard ships during the Transatlantic Slave Trade?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.alt: A labeled diagram of the British slave ship Brookes, showing hundreds of enslaved Africans lying tightly packed on their backs and sides in rows across multiple levels of the ship. Each person is drawn in a uniform, compact space with barely enough room to move. The illustration shows men, women, and children separated and restrained in confined sections.
options.caption: Stowage of the British slave ship Brookes under the regulated slave trade act of 1788. Public domain.

choices.content: They were treated as cargo and tightly packed, demonstrating brutal dehumanization.
The ships provided medical care and periodic relief above deck to reduce suffering.
They were transported in relatively spacious and organized conditions.
Family units were kept together and given adequate room on board.

assessment_items.question_content:
**How did the transatlantic slave trade most significantly impact the economy of Ghana?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: It shifted labor and created a dependency on the capture and sale of enslaved people.
It led to a rapid decline in trade with European nations.
It promoted widespread industrial development and self-sufficiency.
It eliminated regional conflict and unified local kingdoms with a common purpose.

assessment_items.question_content:
**How did the Transatlantic Slave Trade affect Ghana’s court systems?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Courts could send felons to be sold into slavery.
Courts would require a small fine from all convicted people.
Courts were waiving individuals’ offenses if they were classified as free.
Courts ended the practice of enslaving felons.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Who are often considered the overlooked or "hidden victims" of the Transatlantic Slave Trade?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: African communities and families left behind
African slave traders who captured and sold others
European ship captains involved in the slave trade
British soldiers stationed at trading forts like Cape Coast Castle

assessment_items.question_content:
**What limited options did some Ghanaians have when trying to avoid being enslaved during the Transatlantic Slave Trade?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: They could flee their communities or participate in the slave trade themselves.
They could negotiate with slave traders for immunity.
They could commit a crime to be jailed instead of enslaved.
They had no way to avoid enslavement under any circumstances.

assessment_items.question_content:
>The merchants of this country bring bars of iron, copper, cloth, and beads, which they exchange for slaves, gold, and ivory… The kings themselves profit from the trade and sometimes sell their own war captives.

>—Alvise Cadamosto, Venetian explorer describing West African trade along the coast, 1455


**What does this passage suggest about the West African slave trade during the 15th century?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: West African rulers actively participated in and profited from the slave trade.
European merchants forced West African leaders to hand over enslaved people without compensation.
The slave trade was a small, insignificant part of the regional economy.
Slavery was banned by African kings to protect their people from exploitation.

assessment_items.question_content:
>We beg of your Highness to help us in this matter, commanding your factors not to send here either merchants or goods… because it is our will that in these kingdoms there should not be any trade in slaves.  

>—King Afonso I of Kongo, letter to the King of Portugal, 1526


**What does this passage reveal about African leadership responses to the slave trade?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Some African rulers opposed the slave trade and asked Europeans to stop it.
African rulers like Afonso I encouraged slave trading to increase wealth.
King Afonso I welcomed Portuguese traders as long as they paid fairly.
The Portuguese kings were unaware that African rulers disliked the slave trade.
